content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Demographically-driven passive contraction (status-quo baseline)'. Early selection at age 10–12 into parallel 6- and 8-year grammar schools is the primary structural cause of Hungary's current position as Europe's most socially selective education system and should be phased out by 2035 in favor of single-entry-age (14 or 15) and unified secondary-school admission to reduce inequality of opportunity without sacrificing demonstrable value-added in pedagogy.
opportunities: Provides a clean counterfactual baseline against which the incremental effects of S1-S4 can be measured, isolating policy-driven change from demographically-driven change.

Reduces the absolute number of selective-track places over time simply through cohort shrinkage, without requiring any legislative or administrative action, avoiding all the implementation and reversal risks documented for S1-S4.
strengths: Approximately 8–12% of a Hungarian age cohort enters 6- or 8-year grammar school (gimnazium) tracks, selected at age 10 or 12 largely via central written examination; entrants are strongly skewed toward high-SES, urban families.

Since 2022, Hungary has become Europe's most socially selective school system according to PISA; Hungary's social selectivity index worsened by 5.3 points between 2018 and 2022, the largest deterioration in Europe alongside Poland's tracking re-introduction.
threats: Politically the path of least resistance, since it requires no legislative action and therefore faces no direct Fidesz culture-war framing risk, but this apparent political safety is itself a risk: it allows policymakers to avoid the equity problem indefinitely while claiming demographic forces are 'naturally' solving selectivity, when the evidence shows government is simultaneously using score thresholds (S2) as an active supply lever layered atop passive contraction.

Absence of active intervention risks EU-level criticism given the European Commission's explicit 2025 finding that Hungary's system amplifies social and economic inequalities, potentially triggering funds-conditionality pressure regardless of demographic trends.
weaknesses: What is the true causal magnitude of peer effects vs. pedagogical quality in the observed 10% value-added in elite tracks? Current research separates them only weakly.
How much of the performance gap between grammar and vocational students would narrow if school choice and catchment sorting were held constant and only early selection were removed?
